data.title: Julong: A By-The-Numbers Look Ahead Of Its IPO
data.description:
Julong Is Going Public Soon

If you’ve ever yelled at a parking machine in China, odds are Julong (JLHL) built it. And with tariffs bouncing back and forth like ping-pong, Xi and Trump probably taxed you before you even remembered where you parked.

As George Harrison once put it: ‘If you drive a car, I’ll tax the street… if you try to sit, I’ll tax your seat.’

Alright, jokes about the US-China saga aside — Julong’s actually in the business of building smart, integrated systems for utilities, commercial buildings, and multi-family housing in China

A little difficult to understand, isn't it?

Don't worry. I'll give you some examples.

One of Julong’s biggest clients is Beijing Capital Airport, where they handle cloud-based security and video surveillance. That means no bulky in-house servers. Everything’s stored in the cloud, which opens the door for real-time analysis, automation, and even smart cameras that know what (and who) they’re looking at.

Another heavyweight client is Beijing’s public hospital system. There, Julong sets up a central control station that brings together all kinds of building systems. Even managing visitors at a public university in Beijing is part of Julong’s work. They’ve integrated systems

You get the picture by now, don’t you?

Julong obtains these contracts in two ways:

  1. Public tenders (in cases where the client is a government entity like a hospital or university);
  2. Private contracts (when the client is a private entity, this usually covers things like fire protection systems, parking infrastructure, and other similar services).

Most of Julong’s business comes from bidding on Chinese government contracts — in fact, about 86.5% of its FY 2024 revenue (roughly $21.4 million) came from just its top five clients, like public universities, transport hubs, banks, and hospitals.

But what few people realize is that it all started with small-scale products and services for individual customers.

From 1997 to 2012, that was the plan.

Then things took a turn. Julong started focusing on big, complex smart engineering projects and chasing public tenders instead.

Spotting the Good, the Bad, and the Ugly in Julong’s Financials

No one wants to put their money at risk on an IPO of a company that isn’t growing, right? These were the main red flags when I took a look at OFA Group (OFAL), BUUU (BUUU).

Plummeting margins? Yeah, that’s a red flag too. If they’re already struggling to hold onto margins while scaling, just imagine what happens once they’ve gone public and the pressure really kicks in. Agroz (AGRZ) is a great example of this situation. Sweet growth, bitter profits.

Does Julong fit into one of these two groups? Let’s find out.

As a reminder, since the IPO document did not convert the FY 2023 results, we will do it ourselves. I will use the conversion rate of 1 Chinese Yuan as $0.14 US Dollars as it stands today.

In FY 2023, Julong reported sales of $16.67 million, which grew 48.4% year-over-year to $24.74 million in FY 2024. So yeah, Julong’s been putting up solid year-over-year growth.

They also reported a gross margin of 15.8% during FY 2023, falling 50 bp year-over-year to 15.3% in FY 2024. SG&A costs grew 16.3% year-over-year to approximately $917,000. This slight contraction in margins was more than offset by sales growth, taking EBIT from $1.85 million in FY 2023 to $2.8 million in FY 2024, keeping the EBIT margin intact at 11%.

That's 51% year-over-year growth!

On the bottom line, net income climbed from $1.57 million in FY 2023 to $2.4 million in FY 2024 — that’s a 52.8% jump in just one year. With roughly 20 million shares outstanding, EPS grew from $0.07 in FY 2023 to $0.11 in FY 2024.

So far, so good — we’ve got some green flags:

  1. Sales growing in double digits year on year;
  2. Stable margins.

Looking at cash flow, we have a peculiar situation here. During FY 2023, Julong reported a negative OCF of $1.9 million.

The villain? Always him, the terror of high-growth companies: operating assets! In Julong's case, they are bigger as 'Contract Assets', and basically they are amounts that the company is already entitled to receive, but have not yet been officially invoiced. Since they operate in public tenders, this is something they need to manage before their cash runs out.

I recently looked into the IPO of Vistek (VTEK), a company that’s big on infrastructure — mainly underground cable installation in Singapore — and guess what? Contract assets were a hot topic there too.

Same story: long-term projects, revenue recognized before the invoice lands, and that tricky gap between work done and cash in.

This was resolved in FY 2024. With fewer Contract Assets and receivables, Julong posted an OCF of $9.8 million riding the tailwinds of higher net income year over year.

However, there was significant investment cash flow for the acquisition of Julong Online, the operating subsidiary within Mainland China.

This is a bit of a complicated and boring topic, and I consider it one of the red flags here.

Julong Holding, which is going public, is registered in the Cayman Islands—so far so good, this is the traditional structure of a Chinese company that wants to go public, optimizing its tax structure and mitigating regulatory risks.

However, to officially bring Julong Online into the structure, it was necessary to acquire it directly. That's where Beijing Junxinyuan—which was already part of the Julong group—steps in to complete this acquisition.

So, at the end of the day, Julong Holding sits at the top and controls two intermediate entities—Jiangshan and Liyun, both set up in the BVI.

These two, in turn, own a couple of Hong Kong companies: Hong Kong Changfeng and Hong Kong Qinan. And through those, they connect to Beijing Junxinyuan, which is based in mainland China. That's the company that actually holds 99% of Julong Online—the core operating business.

Whew! It's like reading a corporate family tree.

It's a legally creative structure, we can't deny it.

But it works while the winds are favorable. Any change in the regulatory or political climate can bring headaches, and those furthest from the operation feel it first.

These transactions brought their cash balance down from $3.5 million to $2.8 million. But it’s important to note that these were non-recurring events, mainly related to repaying short-term debt.

So, setting aside other types of liabilities, Julong is currently sitting with no short-term or long-term debt on the books.

Valuation Draft

Julong's IPO is still in its preliminary stages. So you're reading this firsthand! In any case, we have a little problem here: the offering has not yet been defined.

That is, we don't know how many shares they will offer, much less the price at which they will offer them.

But we're not totally in the dark.

I’ve followed a few Chinese IPOs before, and based on what’s happening in the market, I have an idea of ​​how much they might be aiming to raise.

I think a raise in the ballpark of $10 to $15 million makes sense, given the growth they've been showing.

If we're looking at a price range of $4 to $6 per share, that would mean about 2 to 3 million shares offered, based on the midpoint of the raise. Another key detail, based on these assumptions, is that total shares outstanding would likely land somewhere between 22 and 23 million.

That's enough to take a look at multiples.

Julong reported sales of $24.74 million in FY 2024, growing over 40% year over year. Let’s be generous here and model at least 50% year over year growth, with sales growth accelerating as they pick up larger contracts. That would give us revenue of $37 million for FY 2025.

With an average of 22 million shares outstanding, Julong would report RPS of $1.68.

That would be a P/S of at least 2.97x. If you’ve been following my IPO series, you’ll notice that this multiple isn’t all that high when you compare Julong to companies that aren’t even growing.

Let's work again with the midpoint scenario of the estimated IPO offering. This would give us an EV of $107 million already discounting the cash on hand from FY 2024. With the revenue I estimated earlier, we would have an EV/S of 2.89x.

Are these sales multiples low?

It depends on your perspective.

Compared to other companies operating in mainland China, such as Tuya (TUYA), a Chinese IoT company focused on smart homes and buildings, Julong would be trading at low multiples in this speculative IPO. Tuya trades at a P/S of 3.61x and EV/S of 1.25x.

Now, when we look at the industry, the median P/S is 2.43x and EV/S is 2.42x. Remember, we are using the midpoint here. If the trading price were $4 and the volume offered was similar to what I projected, we would be slightly below the fair price.

So let’s spell it out: if the offering lands close to my estimates, a $4 price tag would actually be a bit on the low side for a Chinese company in the intelligence and IoT space. But don’t get me wrong, I’m assuming it continues to grow at a stronger pace year over year for FY 2025.

If that doesn’t happen, it will be slightly overpriced on the low end of my estimates.

Since I like to consider the potential midpoint of the IPO offering, $5 would be a bit steep to pay for this Chinese company. Plus, there are a lot of regulatory risks.

That said, I’m going with a ‘Hold’ for now, mainly because we still need more details on the IPO. But just to be clear — Julong already looks more solid than a lot of the Chinese names I’ve been tracking lately.

data.title: Toast: Valuing A Fast Grower
data.description:
Payments processing company Toast, Inc. (TOST) came public in 2021 at a predictably lofty valuation. Despite impressive growth in the subsequent years, the stock is still well below its all-time high. This divergence between stock performance and business performance caught my eye. To assess whether this has provided an opportunity, we need to answer the question of whether Toast’s business prospects remain good, and whether the valuation has compressed enough to become attractive.

Assessing newly public companies is quite difficult both qualitatively and quantitatively. It’s difficult qualitatively because they don’t have a long track record. Capitalism is brutal, and has a way of destroying the best laid plans of mice and men. Without a clear track record, investors need to find some other way of gaining confidence that the company has competitive advantages that will allow it to grow and thrive. It’s difficult qualitatively because newly public companies often have noisy or negative earnings. As an investor that places little faith in EBITDA, preferring GAAP earnings and free cash flow, the process of valuation becomes a little opaque. Nonetheless, I think it can be done. I use a version of Peter Lynch’s valuation method for fast growers, updated for the current investing landscape.

After running Toast through these qualitative and quantitative tests, I think it fits the bill of a company with a bright future and plenty of growth ahead trading at a reasonable valuation. Though the short term may be bumpy, especially with a potential recession depending on the outcome of the current trade negotiations, over the long term the company is positioned to win in a large market.

The Business

Toast is often referred to as a point-of-sale business. That is true: they sell PoS systems and the software that runs them, and they process the payments that flow through them, taking a small cut. But it is more than that. The simplest explanation is the one they give in their 10-K: Toast is “the restaurant operating system, connecting the front of house and back of house operations across service models including dine-in, takeout, delivery, catering, and retail”.

As well as hardware and software for processing orders and payments, Toast provides modules for marketing, creating websites, payroll, inventory management and more. This is how their revenue and gross profit broke down between the three segments in 2024:

Financial technology solutions revenue consists mostly of Toast’s take on the spend that flows through the platform. The costs of revenue for that segment are the fees that Toast pays all the other systems in the value chain, e.g. Mastercard, the issuing bank etc. Gross margin in this segment has been roughly steady in recent years and should remain so going forward.

Subscription services revenue comes from the software that the PoS system uses for which Toast charges a monthly fee, as well as the aforementioned extra modules. Costs in that segment are the maintenance of the cloud infrastructure. As this segment scales, and customers take advantage of more modules, gross margins are increasing, from 60.4% in 2020 to 69% in 2025.

Hardware is the sale of the PoS terminals and tablets, for which the cost is buying the hardware from suppliers. Professional services are setup and training for which the costs are employee salaries. Toast loses money on this segment. Minimising setup costs boosts customer wins and gets more spend on the platform. Gross margin has been improving in recent years, but we shouldn’t expect this to become a source of profit. At best, they will probably run the segment at breakeven.

With a basic understanding of how Toast makes money, I will now apply a framework to analyse how the company can succeed going forward.

Qualitative Analysis

When analysing newly public companies and forecasting their ability to execute growth and profitability plans, I have found it useful to use the framework of Brian Feroldi and his eponymous Feroldi Quality Score. This framework assesses a business on its financial fortitude, moat (the most important section), potential and management to determine whether they have a good chance of delivering on their promises despite all the challenges that capitalism will inevitably put in their way. I don’t necessarily give a score for each metric, but I find the framework useful as a checklist to make sure I’ve considered a business from as many angles as possible. Let’s go through it.

Financial Fortitude

Here we’re looking for a good balance sheet, solid revenues (ideally recurring), high gross margin and profitability on a GAAP and free cash flow basis. Toast has a great balance sheet with $1.4bn in cash and no debt. Most of its revenue is recurring if you combine the “Financial technology services” and “Subscription services” segments, though given restaurant spend goes down in recessions, it’s not rock solid. A revenue benefit is that the payments processing segment captures any upside from food inflation that restaurants pass on to the customer. It has a low gross margin, but it is improving year-on-year as the higher margin subscription services segment is growing faster than the other two. The company has recently become GAAP profitable and continued profitability is expected by analysts going forward. Free cash flow has been positive for two years now, and has grown substantially. Overall, having achieved profitability and with recurring revenue and a fortress balance sheet, Toast is financially strong. The only minor concern is low gross margins and the vulnerability of their customers to a recession.

Moat

This is the key section. Few companies survive very long that don’t have a moat of some sort, and young companies without a moat are particularly vulnerable. So we need to see evidence of one before jumping in.

The first type of moat is network effects - this is where the product improves the more people use it. I see Toast as having a network effect, but a fairly weak one. The fact that many service workers are trained on their system makes it marginally more attractive than some competitors - the more of the market it captures the stronger this effect will get. This can be a very strong moat (e.g. nobody wants to retrain every new hire to use Apple Macs instead of Microsoft) but Toast isn’t ubiquitous enough to score well here (about 13% of US restaurants use it). Additionally, though it takes a little while to train staff on a different system, it’s nowhere near as complex as a computer operating system.

There are some other network effects which are worth mentioning. Because Toast has a lot of restaurants on its platform, it can use the data it collects to improve the user experience. For example, it can benchmark results against local competitors and suggest particular promotions to increase sales. Furthermore, they have great insight into the overall health of the restaurant business. When they provide loans to customers (one of their extra financial services) they have huge amounts of data - more even than a bank - to price that loan by understanding the state of the individual business, as well as the local and national market.

Toast has another dynamic working in its favour which is not technically a network effect but is similar. The company has found that the more restaurants it signs up in a market, the easier sales become. This is because restaurants are generally located near other restaurants (unlike say grocery stores or gas stations which are generally located at a distance from each other). This means that restaurateurs talk to each other and ask about PoS systems. This word of mouth phenomenon leads to what Toast calls “flywheel markets” where sales accelerate with density of customers. This shows up in the numbers: 14% of sales come from customer referrals up from 8% in 2019, and sales rep productivity has increased even as the area each rep covers has gone down:

This is a key advantage when it comes to scale.

Switching costs are fairly high. It is complicated to change your Point of Sale system, and with Toast’s software moving deeper into the workings of a restaurant, it makes it even harder to switch. Many competitors do not have such high switching costs, as they are not built with restaurants specifically in mind and cover only payments processing. It’s a lot easier to change just your PoS system than your PoS system, your payroll provider and your inventory management software all at the same time.

Toast doesn't really have a durable cost advantage although as a larger player in the restaurant vertical they may be able to buy hardware at a lower cost than direct competitors and have their platform costs (e.g. cloud services) spread over a larger user base.

Although Toast certainly has a recognisable brand to restaurateurs, I can’t give it high marks for intangibles assets at least yet.

Counter positioning it does have. This is where competitors would be harmed by adopting a similar business model. This isn’t true of all competitors, for example Lightspeed Commerce, Inc. (LSPD) which already has a similar business model, but it is true for those competitors that don’t have a “restaurant first” solution, i.e. legacy point of sale, or more generalised point of sale solutions. Toast has counter positioning against these competitors because they would have to build a “restaurant first” solution from the ground up to directly compete, which would require R&D investment, dedicated teams and years of fine-tuning to deliver the same service that Toast does today. It’s technically possible, but a tough ask, and companies are more likely to try to bolt on modules to their existing solutions, probably resulting in an inferior product.

Finally, on moats there is pricing power. We might give Toast a little credit for this in that the holistic nature of its offering means it may be able to charge higher prices than competitors, but ultimately, it still needs to compete on price. An example of this came last year when they had to retract a planned $0.99 fee on delivery orders. This remains a very competitive industry and Toast doesn’t have much pricing power.

Toast has some moderate network effects, high switching costs and counter positioning at least against some competitors. Importantly, its moat is also widening as it captures more of its addressable market and adds more products.

Here is a slide from the 2024 Investor Day showing all the products/features Toast has added in the last few years. The deeper they get integrated into the operations of restaurants, the greater their switching costs.

Below is a bar chart showing Toast’s total locations each quarter from 2020 to 2024.

This chart indicates an expanding moat. As Toast takes more and more market share its data becomes more actionable, enhancing the utility of its product. The uniformity of this growth also underlines the robustness of the company’s revenue.

Potential

Since the investment thesis relies on future growth, we need to gain confidence that the opportunity ahead of the company is substantial. As it stands, Toast is used in 134,000 restaurant locations. It estimates the number of restaurants in the US at 875,000, and the number in its “Horizon 1” international locations (including Canada, UK, Ireland) at a further 280,000 for a total of 1.15m. That means their penetration in the restaurant category is less than 12%, so there should be room for further organic growth in locations. One vector of growth is moving upmarket. Currently, Toast serves mostly single restaurants or small local chains, but more corporate sales could help maintain growth over the long term. Recently, Toast has won larger contracts, including with Ascent Hospitality Management to implement Toast Enterprise Solutions at its Perkins American Food Co. and Huddle House brands. That agreement covers 500 restaurants initially.

But Toast has optionality beyond growing locations. As previously mentioned, they are moving beyond the core product of Point of Sale hardware and software, and payments processing. This means greater monetization per location on Toast’s platform, which can be seen in the slide from investor day below, though price increases have also contributed to this:

The level of adoption of new products in Toast’s customer base is not entirely clear, but given these products are pretty new, it’s unlikely to be high. When adding the number of potential future customers and the opportunity to increase average revenue per location, the market opportunity for Toast is very large.

Further to that, there is the possibility that Toast can enter other verticals than restaurants. In fact, this is already a reality with Toast offering solutions tailored to grocery stores. This move makes sense in that many Toast customers operate food service within a retail store, e.g. your local grocery store that has a café. Offering a solution for this vertical means the customer needs only one PoS provider. However, this vertical is not as large as the restaurant vertical, and much of grocery retail is owned by large companies, with a shorter tail of single-store operators. Overall, Toast has a lot of optionality within the restaurant vertical as new products gain adoption, but optionality outside the restaurant vertical is limited. Toast works well because restaurants have such complex needs, whereas existing point of sale solutions are more sufficient for the needs of most other retailers.

Management and Culture

In early stage companies like Toast, it’s important to have quality and committed management to guide the organisation through its growth phase. There are multiple studies that show that founder-led companies outperform the market over time and are more innovative. Companies with moderately high insider ownership also tend to outperform over time. Low ownership means managers are not aligned with shareholders, too high ownership means managers don’t have to pay any attention to shareholders.

Toast is still run by its founders Aman Narang [CEO] and Steve Fredette [President]. The third founder, Jonathan Grimm, no longer works for the company but retains an interest. Those founders own 4.3%, 5.8% and 4.0% of the shares outstanding across A and B shares. That equates to a 14.8%, 18.1% and 12.3% voting interest. This level of insider alignment is good to see. Although the founders do have a substantial voting share, it’s split between three of them, and they can still be outvoted. The managers of the company then have a lot of skin in the game, but don’t have unchallenged control.

There are two ownership issues that shareholders need to keep an eye on - insider selling and dilution. Normally, insider selling is not something I worry about too much. There are many reasons for a director to sell and few of them have anything to do with the fortunes of the company. However, there has been substantial insider selling in the past year or so, including from Stephen Fredette and CFO Elena Gomez. Former CEO Christopher Comparato has sold a lot too, but since he’s stepped down as CEO this is fair enough. A lot of those sold stocks have been replaced by options grants, but that in itself is a problem.

Annual dilution (using the diluted share count) was 4.1% in 2023 and 10.9% in 2024! The company has forecast less than 2% annual dilution going forward, but I am not yet convinced they will achieve that. It’s best to bargain for more.

Finally, Glassdoor ratings suggest a reasonably well-respected management team with 72% approving of the CEO and an overall rating of 3.8. Nothing to write home about but satisfactory for a company that has grown incredibly quickly over recent years.

Overall Toast gets high marks for management and culture. But shareholders need to make sure dilution comes down, and/or factor into their models.

Conclusion on Qualitative Analysis

Having gone through this checklist, I think Toast is a high-quality company, and a potential compounder. It is financially strong with recurring revenue, a healthy balance sheet and improving profitability. It has the beginnings of a moat with its network providing data that improve the product, counter positioning against less restaurant-focussed competitors and legacy systems, and high and increasing switching costs. It operates in a very large market and has plenty of room to grow both locations and revenue per location. The company is founder-led and management is aligned with shareholders.

Concerns to watch out for are the company’s low gross margin (which could lead to swings in bottom-line profitability), the cyclicality of the restaurant industry and shareholder dilution through stock-based compensation.

Risks and Competition

There are two main categories of competitors for Toast: legacy/generalised solutions, and “restaurant first” solutions.

The Toast value proposition against legacy and generalised solutions is clear: restaurants are businesses with specific needs. In very few other operations is the product made, sold and consumed on site - this is a complex process that benefits from the integration of the operations, and the value-added services Toast provides. However, Toast charges for these services, and generalised solutions may work out cheaper, even if they are not as integrated.

Another potential advantage generalised solutions have over Toast is that larger chains often have their own in-house software. This gives them ultimate flexibility, and it means they only need a very simple Point of Sale solution, and none of the value-added services Toast provides. Toast could find it difficult to win much enterprise business.

Toast is also competing with “restaurant first” systems such as Lightspeed and TouchBistro. There are also what I would call “restaurant adapted” products such as Square for Restaurants from Block, Inc. (XYZ) or Clover from Fiserv, Inc. (FI). These options compete with and sometimes surpass Toast on features. Lightspeed, for example, seems to have better inventory management tools and arguably better multi-location support. Clover can be a cheaper solution, and its simplicity and ease of use makes it a good option for some less complex operations. When it comes to NPS score (from comparably.com) and rating on G2, Toast is doing okay but not the best.

Solution

G2 Rating

Toast

Note: I don’t include Square for Restaurants as it’s a small part of Block’s business and I couldn’t find separate NPS scores for that particular product. It scores 4.3 on G2.

I always like to see a high NPS vs competitors but as long as the score is positive and not out of line with the norm, the competitive position should be stable. What makes me worry less about competition from Lightspeed and TouchBistro is that they are a lot smaller than Toast. Lightspeed’s trailing twelve month revenue is $1.05bn (vs $4.9bn for Toast) and growing at a similar rate. TouchBistro is not public, but indications are that it is less than Lightspeed. Lightspeed is growing at a similar rate to Toast.

All this together suggests that although Toast may not have the best solution for all applications, but its solution is good enough to hold and most likely gain market share, when backed up by greater corporate resources for research and development, customer support and sales and marketing. If I were running one of these companies, I would far rather be in Toast’s position. Nonetheless, it’s clear that if Toast stumbles, there are many competitors snapping at their heels, so execution needs to be good.

Beyond competition, Toast faces some more general risks. Although data from the US Census Bureau suggests that restaurant sales are fairly resilient in a recession, tending to go sideways rather than down (with the exception of the pandemic) we must also factor in the possibility that Toast loses customers that go out of business and sees customers that stay cutting back on some valued-added services. Moreover, restaurants that were thinking about changing their PoS system may put off that decision.

Another risk that investors should be made aware of is that Toast misses on earnings, a lot. Although it has beaten in the last four quarters, it missed in all of the previous 10! Hopefully, management has learned a lesson about providing conservative guidance. This may seem a small thing, but it is true that equity outperformance over time is driven by surprise. If companies always performed as analysts (and the market) expected over every time horizon, then every stock would have the same return. Any outperformance or underperformance against the market is then logically driven by the market being surprised at how well or poorly things turn out over the long term. If Toast continues to surprise to the downside, stock outperformance can hardly be expected.

Risks I will watch out for are: a recession and a drop in restaurant sales; Lightspeed growing significantly faster than Toast; and dropping customer satisfaction.

Valuation

My favoured valuation metric for a growth company is Price-Earnings-Growth Ratio [PEG], the one advocated by Peter Lynch. However, this was much more useful in Lynch’s day, because back then, it was almost imperative that a company be profitable by the time it was public. Fast-forward 40 years or so and the business landscape looks very different. In a world that understands network effects and winner-takes-all markets, companies often optimize for growth in the expectation of reaping profits when they have “won”, see the recent history of Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER), or even more famously Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN). As a result, a huge amount of companies don’t turn a profit on a GAAP basis for years after their IPO. HubSpot, Inc. (HUBS) for example, eked out a GAAP profit for the first time in 2024, 10 years after it went public. Nonetheless, it has been an extremely good investment, a 16x since IPO, so it would pay to find some way of adapting the PEG ratio to work for unprofitable companies.

My solution is to create a notional earnings number based on current TTM revenue and what the net profit margin would be if the company were optimising for profitability. Once we have the earnings number as it would have been in Peter Lynch’s world, we compare it to the revenue growth rate. A firm word of caution: this “imaginary” earnings number is doing an awful lot of work in the valuation. It is important therefore, that the investor be very confident in the future of the company in question, confident in their “right to win” (i.e. protective moat), confident in their management and confident that there are no existential threats. Unless the qualitative foundation is very sure indeed, the valuation framework is very speculative.

That’s said, let’s run Toast through the valuation process. The key point is estimating a terminal net margin. One place to start may be the long-term margin targets Toast gave at their 2024 Investor Day:

Unfortunately, these metrics are very confusing. First of all, adjusted EBITDA adjusts out a lot of relevant expenses. Second, it’s not as a percentage of revenue as it would normally be, it’s a percentage of gross profit in two of the three segments (not hardware). Then the GAAP Rule of 40 is not growth plus net margin, it’s growth plus operating profit as a percentage of gross profit in the two “recurring” segments. I think this is too obfuscated to be useful.

To make my own estimate of potential net margin, I’ve estimated the revenue and gross profit mix in 10 years by applying growth rates and gross margin assumptions to each segment:

Growth

Gross Margin

Fintech

Software

25%

75% (improving from 69%)

I have come up with these assumptions based on the 2-year growth rate of each segment (34%, 48% and 20% respectively) and the trend in gross margin for each segment (steady in fintech, improving in software and hardware). This model shows a consolidated gross margin at the end of the period of 35%, up from 24% in 2024.

Now operating expenses. In its first few years as a public company, Toast’s operating expenses grew massively, but in 2024 they got them under control, and they actually declined. I believe operating expenses will resume growth but will decrease as a percentage of revenue over time. This accords with the company’s long-term guidance:

I have operating expenses growing at 10% a year going forward. Applying this and adding a little interest income gets us to a terminal net margin of 17%. Although I think this is achievable long-term, it’s probably prudent to take a lower base case, say 15%. Using this notional net margin and applying it to 2024 and 2025 revenue and growth rates leaves us with the following backward and forward-looking PEG ratios:

Lynch considered between 1-2 PEG normal, above 2 expensive and below 1 a bargain. Using this adapted method, it appears Toast is trading pretty cheaply.

Conclusion

Toast is the largest player in a valuable niche of an enormous market. Although there are many competitors, Toast differentiates itself from legacy and generalised solutions through its specificity for restaurants and value-added services, and from other “restaurant first” competitors through its scale which provides both product benefits (data provides insights, resources for product development) and business benefits (easier sales in flywheel markets). I believe these advantages in network effects, counter positioning and switching costs constitute a narrow but expanding moat. Management’s alignment with shareholders and the fact the company is founder-led increase my confidence that Toast can continue to build its moat, keep growing and achieve my estimate of its potential profitability.

Given my confidence in Toast future business performance, I think it is possible to value it on a PEG ratio basis, using my notional earnings estimate. This notional PEG ratio comes out around 1, making Toast good value if you believe the business can perform as I expect. For this reason, I initiate Toast as a buy.

data.title: Pfizer: Don't Make The Same Mistake Buying This Dip Again
data.description:
There has been little respite (if any) in recent days for Pfizer Inc. (PFE) investors. Just when they thought the hullabaloo over its COVID bubble burst could have seen the worst, the market has had other ideas, as PFE fell to multi-year lows, as sellers “hoodwinked” dip-buyers into catching the proverbial falling knife.

Note that I’ve already cautioned Pfizer investors of this increased plausibility that the underperformance may not be over yet, as I noted a perceptible lack of growth catalysts, amid more profound external risks emanating from uncertain regulatory climate and geopolitical ambiguities.

Therefore, I believe the market reckoning should have been seen a mile away, although one could argue that the downside has been felt throughout the healthcare sector (XLV), as XLV re-tested lows last seen in late 2023. In other words, even the so-called defensive profile of healthcare stocks hasn’t been enough to alleviate the fears of the impending risks imputed to the Trump administration, as the president sets about his plans to remake the global economic order.

While questions about whether it’s even possible have been making waves in the market, as President Trump’s now famous “Blink Wednesday” suggests that the market has likely had enough of his policy whiplash, heightened fears of a financial crisis have already started to creep into the markets.

For Pfizer, such headwinds could not have arrived at the worst possible time. Pfizer clearly needs to show progress not just in its oncology pipeline following its expensive Seagen acquisition, but it is also facing more intense competition brewing in the obesity drugs market, two growth optionalities that Pfizer must seek to overcome its patent cliff challenges over the next few years. However, I believe Pfizer investors have always understood these are long-term prospects that will take some time and gumption for the company to validate its efforts.

Hence, it seems like the unwelcome combo of increased regulatory scrutiny by HHS Secretary RFK Jr.’s alleged skepticism on the benefits of studies supporting the use of vaccines has gained momentum. Coupled with the impending patent cliff challenges that could affect Ibrance, Eliquis over the next few years indicate the timeline and pressure for PFE to prevail in the commercialization of its oncology and obesity drugs portfolio is increasingly pivotal.

Moreover, the recent regulatory perusal on vaccines have also spurred a risk-off posture in other vaccine makers, as the market realizes that RFK Jr.’s could push forward on his vaccine agenda to more intently, potentially inviting unanticipated challenges that might not have been contemplated before. Such is the case that the recent layoffs and changes in key appointment holders within the FDA have worried analysts and investors, intimating we might have to take into account structural changes in the workings of the FDA, including future approvals that could place the commercialization pipeline and timeline at significant risks.

And just when we thought these qualms could have gotten much worse, President Trump’s threat to impose pharma tariffs to “compel” localized manufacturing capabilities back into the US must also be contemplated. Given the recent selloff in healthcare stocks (including PFE), I believe the market has awoken to the severity of the challenges hobbling the proposition of PFE, made worse by the lack of sustained near-term growth catalysts, although the stock has arguably dropped into undervalued levels.

While the pessimism on Pfizer cannot be downplayed, I must also highlight that Pfizer remains a solidly profitable company, although topline growth is expected to stay tepid through 2027. Hence, I believe it's not likely that the stock could attract growth investors seeking an opportunity to buy into the recent crash more than income-seeking investors who are attracted to its forward dividend yield that now closes in on 8%, with a sustainable dividend payout ratio of about 60%.

In other words, income investors should remain on board, although it hasn’t been enough to temper the downside volatility observed in PFE recently. Given the possibly structural headwinds that could affect the cadence of its pipeline, I believe investors are now waking up to the likelihood that such efforts could be delayed post-2028, stretching the patience of investors who arguably have expected an improved business and regulatory environment under President Trump’s leadership.

And if that’s not all, the uncertainties on drug pricing might return to haunt dip buyers again given their profitability, although health insurers have benefited from higher payments.

With that in mind, I’m not surprised that the selling fervor worsened in PFE over the past two weeks, as the stock fell to the $20 level. While the stock is arguably undervalued with a forward P/E of 7.44x (more than 30% below its 10Y average), from a PEG basis, it’s more than 70% over its sector peers.

Hence, I believe the market has taken an even more gloomy outlook on its long-term prospects, as investors take contemplate whether there could be higher execution risks against its pipeline, although PFE has arguably reached an oversold level, helping to mitigate another deeper selloff in the near term.

While income investors might see this as an appealing opportunity to get more involved with the stock, I urge circumspection as the stock’s downtrend bias doesn’t portend well for dip-buyers looking for a possible turnaround.

Although an oversold rally may ensue in the subsequent weeks, it’s better for healthcare investors to search for alternative setups than trying to catch this battered biopharma behemoth.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: The 1-Minute Market Report April 13, 2025
data.description:
In this brief market report, we look at the various asset classes, sectors, equity categories, exchange-traded funds (ETFs), and stocks that moved the market higher and the market segments that defied the trend by moving lower.

Identifying the pockets of strength and weakness allows us to see the direction of significant money flows and their origin.

The tariff pause causes a market rebound

After a four-day slide that saw the S&P 500 index reach correction territory, Trump backed away from most of the reciprocal tariffs that drove the market lower. The rebound on Wednesday was the best day in the market since 2008.

A look at monthly returns

This chart shows the monthly returns for the past year. The negative returns for the last 3 months show how badly the market has reacted to tariff uncertainty.

A closer look at this year’s pullbacks

Here is a closer look at the year-to-date selloffs, using a drawdown chart. After last week’s 5.7% gain, the market is 12.7% off its all-time high.

A look at the bull run since it began last October

This chart highlights the 49.9% gain in the S&P 500 from the October 2022 low through Friday's close. We are now well below the long-term trendline. Analysts’ earnings estimates are coming down and several high-profile companies are no longer providing guidance about future revenue and earnings target, due to the uncertainty they face.

Major asset class performance

Below is a look at the performance of the major asset classes, sorted by last week's returns. I also included the returns from the October 12, 2022 low for additional context.

The Nasdaq index rallied more than any other asset class, thanks in large part to the revival of the Mag 7 stocks. Volatility eased back but is still at an elevated level.

Gold continues to set record highs as investors seek safe haven assets. Bonds would usually rally as a safe haven, but they are instead dropping sharply as foreign investors turn away from US debt.

.

Equity sector performance

For this report, I use the expanded sectors as published by Zacks. They use 16 sectors rather than the standard 11. This gives us added granularity as we survey the winners and losers.

The big winners were Defense contractors and Technology last week. Where did the money to buy these sectors come from? Construction and Real Estate as higher interest rates begin to bite.

Equity group performance

For the groups, I separate the stocks in the S&P 1500 Composite Index by shared characteristics like growth, value, size, cyclical, defensive, and domestic vs. foreign.

The Mag 7 stocks were the best performing equity group, gaining 8.6% on average. Large caps beat Small and Mid-caps.

Foreign Developed stocks had a good week, beating Emerging Market stocks. U.S. Cyclical stocks beat Defensive names.

The S&P Mag 7

Here is a closer look at the seven mega-cap stocks that have been leading the market over the past two years. These seven stocks accounted for 78% of the total YTD gain in the S&P 500 last year. This year they are leading the way down. Nvidia (NVDA) had a particularly good week.

The S&P Top 7 are leading the S&P 500 index lower

The 10 best-performing ETFs from last week

Gold Miners led the pack last week, with Semiconductors not far behind. Nuclear Energy and Space Exploration also did very well.

The 10 worst-performing ETFs from last week

China stocks led the way down, losing 4.6% for the week. Solar Energy also saw heavy selling as higher interest rates make it more expensive for consumers to install panels on their rooftops.

Final thoughts

The equity market is trying to dig itself out of the hole that was created by the tariff tantrum. It’s making progress, but it still has a long way to go. Volatility remains high, and interest rates are spiking higher. The odds of a recession have gone up significantly, as have the odds of a bear market.

I’m often asked why tariffs are causing such angst among investors and consumers? The simplest answer is that the size and scope of the announced tariffs are so large that businesses don’t know what to expect as they prepare for the coming months and years. Should they bring their manufacturing plants back to the U.S.? That would be a huge expense, and the tariffs might end up being negotiated away. It’s this uncertainty that has everyone so upset, and Trump has now extended the uncertainty for another 90 days with his pause announcement.

No doubt the U.S. will get some concessions from other countries, but at what cost? We just don’t know the answer, and that is the problem.

To recap last week’s action, investors were:

  • Buying gold and tech and selling bonds and volatility.
  • Buying domestic and selling foreign stocks.
  • Buying Large Cap stocks and selling Small and Mid-Caps.
  • Buying Tech and Defense Contractors and selling Construction and Real Estate.
  • Buying Large Cap Growth and selling Small-Cap Value.
  • Buying Nuclear Energy and selling Solar Energy.

data.title: Azelis: Correction Puts The Stock In The 'Buy' Zone
data.description:
Introduction

Belgium-based Azelis (OTCPK:AZLGF) (OTCPK:AZLGY) is a service provider to the food industry where it helps solving the issues its customers have by, for example, improving the taste of medication and food supplements, or making soy milk or almond milk tastier.

Azelis has its primary listing on Euronext Brussels, where it was recently added to the BEL-20 index. The company is trading with AZE as its ticker symbol, and considering the average daily volume is currently around a quarter of a million shares per day, I would strongly recommend to trade in Azelis' shares using the facilities of Euronext Brussels. As there are approximately 244M shares outstanding (rounded), the current market cap is approximately 3.7B EUR.

The stock wasn’t cheap before the market correction, but now it is

The total revenue generated by Azelis in 2024 was approximately 4.24B EUR, which is approximately 1.5% higher than in 2023. Fortunately, its COGS didn’t increase at the same pace as we only saw a 0.5% increase, and this helped boost the gross profit by 47M EUR, which is almost 5% higher than the 2023 gross profit.

The other expenses did increase at a faster pace, and that’s why the operating profit remained relatively stable (a small decrease of less than 0.5%). And that means it really is the lower net finance cost that pushed the pre-tax income higher, while the net income on a consolidated basis remained unchanged at 189.5M EUR. But in 2024, a larger portion of that net income was attributable to the shareholders of Azelis. The attributable net income increased to 180.7M EUR, which represented 0.74 EUR per share.

This didn’t really justify a share price of around 20 EUR per share, but due to all the acquisitions, Azelis has to deal with a relatively high amortization rate of the intangible assets it acquires during those acquisitions. It, for instance, amortizes the distribution rights, some of the development costs and the value of customer lists. These are of course non-cash items, and it’s important to see how much cash flow the core business is generating. That cash flow is subsequently used for M&A (which increases the amount of intangible assets on the balance sheet again, and keeps the amortization at a relatively high level).

In 2024, the company reported an operating cash flow of 323M EUR. This includes a 133M EUR investment in working capital items, but it also excludes the 122M EUR in interest payments and the 38M EUR in lease payments. On top of that, the total amount of taxes owed was about 5M EUR higher than the actual cash tax outflow.

This means the adjusted operating cash flow was approximately 291M EUR. And as the cash flow statement above shows, the total capex was just under 14M EUR, resulting in an underlying free cash flow of approximately 277M EUR. That cash inflow was used on acquisitions (241M EUR) and the purchase of non-controlling interests (53M EUR). Azelis also paid a dividend of 53.3M EUR, which represented 0.22 EUR per share. The FY 2024 dividend (payable in 2025) will be 0.23 EUR (proposed).

The underlying free cash flow of 277M EUR represents approximately 1.14 EUR per share using the current share count of 243.5M shares. And that makes the current share price more appealing. The difference between EPS and FCFPS is predominantly caused by the higher depreciation and amortization expenses (115M EUR) versus the total amount of capex and lease payments (52M EUR).

Exposure To US Tariffs Should Remain Limited

As disclosed in the annual report, the company generated total revenue of 4.21B EUR, of which about 1.54B EUR was realized in The Americas. While the company has not fully broken down its exposure to the US, plenty of other countries in North and South America contributed to that revenue result.

Additionally, the company has in excess of 1,100 employees in the Americas, where it also operates 23 offices and 17 labs. This means Azelis should be able to shift a portion (if not all) of its US-driven work to the United States. As such, I’m not particularly afraid of the fall-out caused by the tariffs (as Azelis should also be able to pass on a substantial portion of those expenses to its US-based customers), and the general slowdown of the world economy likely is a bigger factor to keep in mind.

Azelis also mentioned it sees a recovery in many of its end markets as the market sentiment is growing, and it specifically highlighted the Americas, where its positive momentum is expected to continue.

Investment thesis

The market correction has made plenty of stocks cheaper and more attractive, but despite having encountered a few tougher years, Azelis still is a cash flow machine. The increase in working capital positions was timed ahead of an anticipated revenue increase, as Azelis’ order book was developing well.

Looking at the analyst consensus estimates for the next few years, the EBITDA is expected to increase by 20% in the next three years, which should result in an attractive net profit increase to 303M EUR in 2027 as per the analyst consensus (the net income will be boosted by the anticipated lower net interest expenses as well). Of course, those consensus estimates will likely have to be fine-tuned in the near future once it becomes somewhat more clear what Azelis’ net exposure is to US tariffs. I think the slowdown of the world economy is a bigger threat to Azelis’ future than the tariffs.

I currently have no position in Azelis, but the company is currently trading at an attractive valuation.

Longleaf Partners International Fund returned +0.73% in the first quarter, trailing the relevant indexes. Index performance was driven by strong returns in the European market fuelled by the promise of debt funded fiscal stimulus by Eurozone economies, potentially benefiting local Defense, Energy, and Industrial sectors (as discussed below). There was a large dispersion in returns between large-cap vs. small and mid-cap (SMID), and between stimulus beneficiary sectors vs. the rest. Our overweight in Consumer sectors and underweight in European Industrials, Financials, Energy sectors largely explains the majority of our relative underperformance.

In Asia, lacking the stimulus support (or hope thereof) and reflecting the geopolitical and tariff uncertainty, key markets including Japan and Australia were down in the quarter. A notable exception was China / Hong Kong, which staged a strong comeback in the first quarter, outperforming the US market by the largest margin since 2007. This was primarily driven by optimism around Chinese technology stocks post DeepSeek model release, stimulus measures, regulatory support and inexpensive valuations.

In Europe, the quarter was dominated by two themes. Firstly, the perception of a reproachment between the Trump administration and Russia, seemingly at the expense of the traditional democratic alliance of Canada, Europe and the UK. Secondly, the overarching threat of US tariffs. Taken at face value, you would expect both of these to have a profoundly destabilizing impact on investor sentiment and would likely predict a negative stock market return. How, then, to explain Europe’s STOXX 600 positive return of +5.9% in the quarter? The answer is to be found in the response of European politicians. Seemingly jolted from beneath their comfort blanket of US protection, there has been a flurry of calls for investment into European rearmament via increased defense budgets and into support for industrial and energy infrastructure spending (particularly in Germany). The EU has a mixed track-record of converting lofty ambitions into on-the-ground activity, so whether the rhetoric translates into real-world spending, we will have to wait to see, but in Q1, it was the projection of solidarity which mattered most.

Yet, the performance at the index level may be misleading and skewed by the extraordinary share price performance of a relatively small number of perceived beneficiaries. These naturally included Aerospace and Defense companies, as well as a number of German industrials. But it also included gold-related stocks, with the gold price responding to the raised volatility in a more predictable way, alongside financials with the promise of credit growth and higher interest rates, and traditional risk-averse assets such as utilities.

This potentially misleading index performance becomes evident when we look more closely at the three largest European markets, UK, France and Germany. Take the UK as an example, while the index of the largest 100 stocks (the FTSE100) had a positive performance of +6.1%, the indexes for the next largest 500 stocks (the FTSE 250 and FTSE Small-Cap) were both negative (-5.0% and -4.2% respectively). And yet, the FTSE All-Share index (which is these 600 stocks combined) still had a positive performance not far off the FTSE100 index (+4.5%). This demonstrates the strong outperformance of large-caps (which prevailed across all European markets) and reaffirms how skewed indexes are to their largest constituents.

Indeed, the majority of stocks in these three key markets underperformed their indexes, with the minority outperformers heavily skewed to large caps. These unbalanced skews in terms of extreme performance and large-cap bias account for the unusual situation where the index performance can confound rational expectations, but the underlying performance of the majority of stocks was, more predictably, negative.

The reliance on a minority of stocks to drive outperformance lends a higher degree of fragility to the index performance overall. We have started to see a rapid reversal in the first weeks of April following the US tariff announcement, with many of those standout share price performers in Q1 being down the most heavily so far in Q2.

Our prudent, bottom-up approach to stock-picking, looking for high-quality businesses trading at a significant discount to their fair value, often leads us to the lesser-explored corners of markets. That includes mid-caps and under-appreciated business models or industries which, as we have attempted to show above, were largely underperformers in the quarter. While the share prices of some of our investments disappointed, the performance of the businesses themselves and their leadership teams did not, barring a couple of exceptions we discuss in more detail below.

We would suggest that index performance is always a crude measure of circumstance, hiding a wealth of complexity. Taking a step back and considering the main geopolitical and macroeconomic headlines during the quarter, would we have predicted a positive return for the portfolio (or indexes) all other things being equal? Probably not. The fact that our portfolio constituents were able to generate a positive overall return under such circumstances indicates the resilience of the businesses we own, and the large margin of safety baked into our positions.

Impact of US Trade Tariffs on our Portfolio:

On April 2nd, President Trump announced "reciprocal" tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), citing a national emergency related to foreign trade practices. A baseline 10% tariff on imports from all countries took effect on April 5th, followed by higher tariffs for countries with unfair trade practices, effective April 9th. Specific rates include 34% for China (on top of 20% imposed earlier this year), 20% for the European Union, 46% for Vietnam, 26% for India, 24% for Japan, and 10% for the UK. This amounts to a simultaneous trade war against all the key economies globally and has had the predictable effect of a sharp selloff in equity markets globally, especially in names exposed to global trade with the US.

US exceptionalism, driven by mega-cap US technology stocks, is also starting to crack. The Old-World order is getting reorganized under US President Trump. Countries that have long been allied with the US are being alienated, and this has the potential to create strange bedfellows.

While the rapid market selloff has been broad-based, which has impacted our portfolio, the majority of our portfolio companies cater to predominantly domestic consumption and are largely insulated from direct tariff impact. This has been a deliberate decision over the past few years. We consciously repositioned the portfolio to more domestically focused names to avoid the inherent risks and uncertainties that geopolitics has always posed, particularly in relation to our China and broader Asia exposure. We exited Man Wah during first quarter partly driven by this objective (given their high exposure to US sales sourced from Vietnam).

We want to own businesses which have a greater control over their own prospects, through the ability to manage the variables of supply, demand and capital allocation inherent to successful value creation. The companies in our portfolio with significant US exposure to physical products sales typically either manufacture locally or have a product we believe is hard to replicate domestically.

Glanbia (OTCPK:GLAPF)(OTCPK:GLAPY) generates 80%+ of revenue in the US. Virtually all of these products are produced domestically, including the inputs (largely whey) and therefore not subject to any direct tariff impact.

Samsonite (OTCPK:SMSOF) is the most exposed company in our portfolio to direct tariff impacts, as it generates 36% of its revenue from the US. Of its US product, 15% is sourced from China, while the remaining 85% is sourced from ASEAN countries, including Thailand, Cambodia, Indonesia, Vietnam, and the Philippines.

Becle (OTCPK:BCCLF), the leading manufacturer of tequila, exports tequila to the US as well as Irish whiskey from Ireland. Tequila is part of the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) list, which has so far avoided a worst-case tariff hit.

Gruma (OTCPK:GMKKY)(OTCPK:GPAGF), the world’s leading tortilla maker with nearly 80% of its value in the US, follows a local production for local consumption strategy, and therefore does not have material tariff risk.

Lanxess (OTCPK:LNXSF)(OTCPK:LNXSY), a global speciality chemical company, derives approximately 30% of revenue from the US, predominantly from local production. It is, of course, nonetheless exposed to secondary impacts, likely to include end-market demand weakness.

Notable Contributors and Detractors

Millicom (TIGO)(OTCPK:MLCMF)– Latin American telecommunications operator Millicom extended its standout 2024 performance into the first quarter of this year. The share price reflects exceptional operational delivery, driving an inflection in equity free cash flow growth, on which the new management team, supported by its largest shareholder, Iliad Group (Atlas) with a 40% stake, has relentlessly focused since 2023. This progress became more visible starting in the second quarter of 2024, and we expect it to continue from here.

We were confident the market would eventually recognize the material year-on-year (YoY) and sequential free cash flow growth profile that Millicom’s results would gradually reveal. Indeed, the company exceeded its already twice-raised 2024 equity free cash flow guidance of approximately $650 million, ultimately delivering $728 million for the year. Looking ahead, Millicom has guided further growth to the $750 million range for 2025. Our conversations with management bolster our confidence in the sustainability of this strong cash flow generation, underpinned by efficiency gains across both OPEX and CAPEX initiatives.

The restart of the company’s dividend policy followed the achievement of its steady-state leverage target of 2.5x in December. This capital allocation move makes cash flow improvements very tangible to us as shareholders today, thanks to the ongoing $150 million share buyback program and a well-supported dividend of $3 per share in hard currency. At current prices, this represents a yield above 10%, limiting downside risk in today’s highly volatile markets and significantly improving the risk/reward of our investment in Millicom. In the meantime, we continue to partner with management while they drive operational improvements to full fruition over 2025.

In addition to this compelling fundamental setup, we continue to see multiple ways to win in what promises to be a catalyst-rich year for Millicom. The delisting of its Swedish shares was completed at the end of March 2025, leaving a single US listing that should enhance liquidity and attract greater institutional investor interest—further facilitating the closure of Millicom’s price-to-value gap. Meanwhile, progress on the $1 billion acquisition of Telefonica’s operations and Millicom's minority interests in Colombia remains on track. Management recently confirmed that they expect regulatory approval by year-end, and they remain excited about the prospect of generating value on these assets via operational turnaround and cost synergies.

Prosus (OTCPK:PROSY)(OTCPK:PROSF) - Global consumer internet group Prosus was among top contributors for the quarter, continuing its strong performance from 2024. Tencent, which represents close to 80% of net asset value (NAV) for Prosus, delivered better than expected results in Q4. The company reported YoY revenue growth of 11% and adjusted net profit increase of 30%, benefiting from improved operational leverage. Tencent is stepping up its investment in AI and is adopting a multi-model strategy (including DeepSeek) to provide the best user experience. Its internally developed Hunyuan model is helping boost ad targeting, content creation and operational efficiency. We remain optimistic about Tencent's investments in AI yielding a high return on investment at the application layer, particularly given the vast amount of data generated by their WeChat super app and its 1.3 billion monthly active users. Prosus continued its open-ended share buyback program and announced two reasonably priced acquisitions (Just Eat Takeaway (OTCPK:JTKWY)(OTCPK:TKAYF) in food delivery and Despegar (DESP) in online travel) for a total of $6 billion (~3% of its NAV).

Lanxess – Global specialty chemical company Lanxess was one of the top performers in the quarter, being a clear beneficiary of both macro drivers and internal initiatives. We have argued for some time that Lanxess was undervalued in relation to the quality of its assets and the actions management was taking to allow for significantly improved performance and returns once the end-demand environment started to show signs of improvement. Yet ironically it was the macro prospect of an ending of the war in Ukraine, followed by the expectation of renewed investment into German industrial capabilities, which saw Lanxess shares perform strongly this quarter. Lanxess has approximately 25% production exposure to Germany and a mid-teen demand exposure. Lower gas prices in Germany would certainly help with input costs, but we remain cautious about just how much Russian gas will be allowed to flow through Ukraine and how much Germany will want to tie itself back into a Russian supply network. The €500 billion infrastructure blitz proposed by new Chancellor Merz would certainly help the domestic construction market, but again, we need to see evidence of this on the ground.

We prefer to rely on levers within the company’s control, and Lanxess made further progress through the quarter. The sale of the Urethanes division completed as expected with gross proceeds of €500 million, which will be used to reduce debt back to an approximate 3x leverage ratio. Q4 results were good, with cost savings on track and more to be delivered this year. Guidance for 2025 was conservative at +10% earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA), but prudent given the end-market environment has remained weaker for longer than they would normally expect. There is significant upside margin potential in the coming years as demand feeds directly through to utilization improvements with a very high incremental margin. We are also getting closer to the sale of their stake in the Envalior JV (put option from Apr 2026). This asset and its shareholder loan are worth over 35% of Lanxess’s market cap today, yet analysts and the market largely ignore it or have written it down to a very low valuation. This reflects the fact Lanxess is strictly limited in what they are allowed to disclose about Envalior, and markets abhor a vacuum. Yet the debt agency reports (S&P Global, 13 Dec 2024 publicly available online) make it clear what this asset is worth if one cares to look. With a fixed exit-multiple of 12x EBITDA guaranteed in the put option, we are confident a strong cash inflow will be achieved on the sale, allowing for significant further debt reduction and finally opening the possibility of accretive buybacks.

Richemont (OTCPK:CFRHF)(OTCPK:CFRUY) - Swiss jewellery and watch producer Richemont was another top performer in the quarter. Their fiscal Q3 results (calendar Q4) released in mid-January again demonstrated the brand strength which enables pricing power and market share gains. Like-for-like sales growth of 10% substantially outperformed expectations, with the US being the standout geography, but with strength across the board except for China.

With the prospect for YoY comparisons easing further through the year (and in January at least, consumer prospects seemingly strong) the share price strongly outperformed our value growth. This reduced our IRR expectation, and we started to trim the position as the price to value approached 100%. We ultimately exited our position fully in the quarter, which proved to be the right decision as the market became more concerned about consumer weakness and the impact of tariffs in the US, seeing the share price pull back.

We continue to like Richemont, as owner of the strongest brands in the hard-luxury industry, Cartier and Van Cleef & Arpels. We have long-held a preference for hard luxury (jewellery and watches) over soft luxury (bags, clothes) given the long-term store- of-wealth characteristics and occasion-linked purchasing (engagements, birthdays, anniversaries, etc.) Hard luxury globally has significantly lower penetration of brands than soft luxury, with many emerging markets still being precious metal/stone dependent rather than brand/design dependent. We continue to expect brands to win market share, disproportionately favouring the most desirable brands, providing Richemont with a long-term structural tailwind. We will continue to follow the company closely.

Glanbia - Irish sports nutrition and ingredients company Glanbia was a top detractor in the quarter. The shares had already performed poorly in H2 2024, as the price of their key input cost for their performance nutrition brands, whey protein isolate (WPI), rose above historic peaks. There is a shortage of WPI supply, but with new capacity due to come online later this year, the company expected prices to remain rational and ultimately to reduce. That did not happen, and through December and into January, prices rose further, forcing Glanbia to forward-purchase at prices far above their budgeted costs. Glanbia is simultaneously the largest consumer of WPI and the largest producer, giving them the best insight into this commodity market, and even they were caught out given the unprecedented nature of the price move. Despite finding mitigations that allowed them to offset 75% of the extra $200 million cost headwind for this year, it still resulted in a $50 million downgrade to guidance for 2025 at the FY results. That is a 9% downgrade to consensus expectations, with the share price falling over 3x that. An aggravating factor has been concerns about increased competition.

Costco (COST) is an important channel for Glanbia, accounting for a mid-teen percentage of sales, but being more substantial as a growth driver. Costco introduced a competitor product under their Kirkland brand in Q3 last year, which has caused a headwind to growth for Glanbia’s key Optimum Nutrition (ON) protein powder. Performance so far has been stable, impacting ON volumes but not showing progressive deterioration, yet it introduces a further concern to an already nervous market.

Glanbia’s other division, Nutritional Solutions, is the largest producer of WPI and therefore benefits from the current high prices, but the market has historically focused solely on the consumer branded division, and that has continued to be the case.

Looking at the value of its assets, Glanbia now on 9x price-to-earnings (PE), trades well below comparators for each of their divisions: Sports Nutrition Brands, Supplements & Ingredients, and Dairy/Cheese producers. We have impressed upon the Board and Management their vulnerability at the current share price and the need to respond with affirmative action. That would include enlarging and accelerating share buybacks and exploring a strategic process to separate and sell certain divisions. We believe the company is considering such a strategy, having already announced the reorganisation into three divisions, which would be a necessary precursor to a sale of the new Dairy Protein division. We believe that business alone is worth approximately 50% of the company enterprise value (EV) today, with the more valuable consumer brands (GPN) and ingredients (Health & Nutrition) also being potential sale targets. We continue to like the structural drivers of growth in Glanbia’s health-focused businesses and are working with management to ensure a higher degree of urgency in crystalising value.

Becle - Global beverage company Becle was a detractor for the quarter. Industry trends remained weak, and while the company gave guidance that it can get back to growth this year, the market is skeptical. Last quarter, we wrote: “While it remains to be seen what Trump will do, Becle is trading dramatically below its private market value and below where it has traded before on all metrics as a public company, including the first time we successfully invested in the company when Trump was President. 2025 will be an important year for the company to demonstrate its path to growth and prudent capital allocation.” Throughout most of the first quarter, the situation looked precarious as Trump initially focused his tariffs on Canada and Mexico. However, after quarter end the tequila industry received a reprieve as we wrote above. We continue to believe that Becle has multiple factors within its control to realize value per share and look forward to continuing our dialog with management.

Jollibee (OTCPK:JBFCF)(OTCPK:JBFCY)- The largest quick-service restaurant (QSR) player in the Philippines, Jollibee, was a detractor for the quarter. The company reported disappointing results for Q4, missing its earnings expectations. While its domestic business in the Philippines continues to perform well, with same-store sales growth (SSSG) increasing from 6.4% in the previous quarter to 7.4% in the most recent one, the Group's overall performance was negatively impacted by its international operations, particularly in the China region and with Smashburger.

In China, EBITDA fell 57% YoY due to a challenging competitive environment and a weak consumer market. Meanwhile, in the US, Smashburger's EBITDA returned to negative territory as the revamp of the menu and increased marketing spending did not yield the desired results. Jollibee is actively implementing changes in these underperforming markets and brands. The company plans to limit losses in China by focusing on an asset-light approach. Additionally, Jollibee has changed the management of Smashburger and is pursuing a more cost-conscious strategy under the new President, Jim Sullivan, who is working to eliminate unnecessary overhead.

Jollibee Group remains focused on return on invested capital (ROIC), intending to raise it to 20% by fiscal year 2028. Encouragingly, they are seeing positive signs with the launch of Jollibee's US Franchising Program, set for early March 2025. The Jollibee brand in North America reported an 8% increase in same-store sales last year and has average daily sales (ADS) of $13,300, nearly triple the ADS of KFC and double that of Popeyes in the US. Additionally, Jollibee Group is ready to begin franchising Coffee Bean & Tea Leaf beyond its core markets of Malaysia and Singapore.

Samsonite - Samsonite, a global luggage manufacturer, was a detractor for the quarter. While Samsonite’s 4Q24 results beat consensus, its weak 1Q25 guidance of low-to-mid single-digit decline weighed on its share price. In 4Q24, Samsonite grew revenue by +1.0% YoY on a constant currency basis (cFX). This was driven by continued weakness in its Asia region, with revenue declining 6.3% YoY, on a constant FX-basis. In Asia, China continues to suffer from the weak consumption environment, and India has been impacted by the aggressive competitive landscape. Management continues to enforce their price discipline to uphold the integrity of its brand for the longer term, with gross margin and adjusted EBITDA holding strong at 60.2% and 20.7%, respectively. On its 1Q25 guidance, the weak outlook was attributable to its Asia and North America regions. North America saw dampened consumer sentiment from the increasingly volatile geopolitical landscape while Asia suffered from a high base effect, particularly in China, where 1Q24 saw an extremely strong Chinese New Year. On the positive, management continues to aggressively engage in share buybacks and is actively preparing for its dual listing in the US (subject to market conditions, which have not been conducive lately).

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter we did not initiate any new positions in the portfolio. However, we did exit three positions, Havas (OTC:HAVSF) (from the Vivendi spin at the end of last quarter), Man Wah and Richemont. Following quarter end, in the wake of Trump’s “Liberation Day” announcements, we are finding more opportunities to continue upgrading the portfolio’s quality and margin of safety. Our on-deck list is growing fast, and we plan to take action, in a measured way, to deploy our cash balance from quarter end.

Outlook

We remain excited about 2025 and beyond. The businesses we own are making solid operational progress and are well-positioned to go on offense, even as the ripple effects of the newly implemented tariffs bring new challenges and uncertainty. With the Fund’s price-to-value ratio in the mid-60s% we believe there is substantial opportunity in the portfolio today. The Fund’s non-US portfolio managers have been increasing their own investments in the portfolio given the attractive business – people – price opportunity we see today. Our cash position grew over the quarter based on the exits listed above but has decreased some in April as we have put money to work to improve our margin of safety (including one new position).
